stem: A company runs an Aurora MySQL cluster with one writer and three read replicas. The application uses the cluster endpoint for writes and the reader endpoint for reads. During a failover, the application experiences 45 seconds of write downtime. The team needs to reduce this to under 10 seconds. Which Aurora feature reduces failover time?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable Aurora Multi-Master to have multiple writers simultaneously

options.label: B
options.text: Create an RDS read replica in a different region and promote it during failover

options.label: C
options.text: Use the Aurora Global Database with a secondary region for failover

options.label: D
options.text: Enable Aurora Fast Failover — Aurora promotes the replica with the least replication lag.

explanation: Aurora Fast Failover typically completes DNS propagation in 30-120 seconds. To get under 10 seconds, use RDS Proxy: the proxy maintains connection pooling and retries automatically during the Aurora failover window — the application connects to the proxy endpoint (which is stable) and the proxy handles reconnection to the new writer. Aurora Multi-Master was deprecated for MySQL. Aurora Global Database provides cross-region DR but doesn't reduce single-region failover time. RDS read replicas outside Aurora don't benefit from Aurora's fast failover mechanism.

stem: A company uses SQS with a Lambda consumer. The Lambda function processes messages and occasionally fails on specific malformed messages. The DLQ is configured with maxReceiveCount=5. A message fails 5 times and moves to the DLQ. However, the Lambda function continues processing because the DLQ is a separate SQS queue that Lambda is also consuming from the same event source mapping. Messages in the DLQ keep failing and recirculating. What architectural change prevents recirculation?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase maxReceiveCount to 10 to give more retry attempts before DLQ

options.label: B
options.text: Set the DLQ visibility timeout to 24 hours to slow down recirculation

options.label: C
options.text: Enable SQS FIFO for the DLQ to prevent duplicate processing

options.label: D
options.text: Remove the Lambda event source mapping for the DLQ; consume the DLQ separately.

explanation: The problem is that Lambda is consuming BOTH the main queue AND the DLQ from the same event source mapping (or separate mappings). When messages fail in the DLQ consumer, they could recirculate if the DLQ itself has no downstream DLQ. The fix: remove the Lambda event source mapping for the DLQ. The DLQ should be consumed by a separate process for human review, debugging, or a purpose-built analysis Lambda that logs/parses failures without throwing exceptions (which would re-queue in the DLQ). Increasing maxReceiveCount just delays the problem. Visibility timeout slows but doesn't prevent recirculation. FIFO doesn't prevent DLQ recirculation.

stem: A company uses CloudFormation to deploy infrastructure. A new stack update modifies an RDS instance's DBInstanceClass (instance size change). During the update, the RDS instance becomes unavailable for 5 minutes. The team did not expect downtime. What CloudFormation behavior caused this and how should they have avoided it?

options.label: A
options.text: Modifying DBInstanceClass in CloudFormation triggers an in-place instance class change with brief downtime — not a resource replacement. Data is preserved on the existing storage.

options.label: B
options.text: CloudFormation performed a replacement update instead of an in-place modification; the team should have used a ChangSet to review the update type before execution

options.label: C
options.text: RDS DBInstanceClass changes are always disruptive; there is no way to avoid downtime

options.label: D
options.text: The team should have used RDS Blue/Green Deployments for the instance size change

explanation: In CloudFormation, RDS DBInstanceClass is an Update with Some Interruption property — it resizes the instance in-place with a brief restart, but does NOT destroy and recreate the instance. No data migration is required. Properties that DO cause replacement include DBInstanceIdentifier (when changed), Engine, and MultiAZ (in some cases). DeletionPolicy: Retain protects the RDS instance from being deleted if the stack is deleted, but does not affect update behavior. The correct approach for zero-downtime resizing is to use a Blue/Green deployment or an RDS read replica promotion.

stem: A company uses Route 53 with a complex routing configuration: latency routing at the top level, weighted routing at the second level, and health checks on all endpoints. During a partial failure of the us-east-1 region (some endpoints unhealthy), some users in the US still get routed to unhealthy endpoints. What is the most likely configuration issue?

options.label: A
options.text: Weighted routing always distributes to all healthy records — there is no failover within a weighted record set

options.label: B
options.text: Route 53 latency routing does not support health checks

options.label: C
options.text: The weighted routing records reference health checks, but the health check is evaluating the wrong port

options.label: D
options.text: Route 53 health checks are not configured for the weighted routing records

explanation: Route 53 only fails over to other records when health checks are associated with the specific routing record. In a latency → weighted routing chain: if the weighted records under us-east-1 don't have health checks configured, Route 53 will continue to include unhealthy endpoints in the weighted distribution. Health checks must be configured at the weighted record level (not just the latency level) to remove unhealthy endpoints from the rotation. Option D is wrong — weighted routing DOES skip unhealthy records when health checks are configured. The issue is missing health checks on the weighted tier.

stem: A company's application uses DynamoDB global tables replicated across us-east-1 and eu-west-1. A bug in the us-east-1 application writes corrupted data to 50,000 items. These writes replicate to eu-west-1 within seconds. The team needs to restore the 50,000 items to their pre-corruption state. What is the correct recovery approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Global tables have automatic conflict resolution — the older write wins and the data will self-correct

options.label: B
options.text: Use DynamoDB PITR to restore to a new table at the point before corruption

options.label: C
options.text: Use DynamoDB Streams in eu-west-1 to replay pre-corruption events

options.label: D
options.text: Disable global table replication, restore eu-west-1 from backup, re-enable replication

explanation: PITR (Point-in-Time Recovery) allows restoring to any second within the last 35 days. The team restores the DynamoDB table (or uses selective restore for specific items using ExportTableToPointInTime to S3, then import) to a NEW table at a timestamp before the bug ran. They then read the 50,000 correct item values from the restored table and write them back to the live global table. This corrects both regions (global table replication propagates the fixes). Disabling replication is complex and risks more data inconsistency. Streams contain the corrupted events. Global table LWW conflict resolution is based on timestamps — if corrupted data has a later timestamp, it wins.

stem: A company runs a containerized application on EKS. The cluster uses managed node groups. The team deploys a new application version and discovers a critical bug. They need to roll back to the previous deployment version within 2 minutes. kubectl is available. What is the fastest rollback method?

options.label: A
options.text: Scale the current deployment to 0 replicas and scale up the previous deployment to the original replica count

options.label: B
options.text: Delete the EKS node group and recreate it with the previous AMI

options.label: C
options.text: Run kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name> to roll back to the previous ReplicaSet

options.label: D
options.text: Delete the current deployment and re-apply the previous deployment YAML from source control

explanation: `kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name>` instantly rolls back to the previous ReplicaSet (which Kubernetes maintains by default, keeping the last 10 revisions). The rollback completes in seconds as Kubernetes shifts traffic from the new pods to the old ReplicaSet's pods. This is the purpose-built Kubernetes rollback mechanism. Re-applying YAML from source control requires locating the previous version and reapplying — slower. Scaling manually is error-prone. Deleting/recreating node groups is a cluster-level operation that would cause much longer downtime and is completely unrelated to application rollback.

stem: A company has a CloudFormation stack with a DeletionPolicy of Retain on all resources. A new developer accidentally runs aws cloudformation delete-stack. What happens to the resources?

options.label: A
options.text: The stack is deleted from CloudFormation but all resources remain running and are no longer managed.

options.label: B
options.text: All resources are permanently deleted along with the stack

options.label: C
options.text: The deletion is blocked until the DeletionPolicy is changed

options.label: D
options.text: Resources are moved to a 'detached' state and remain in CloudFormation with no stack association

explanation: DeletionPolicy: Retain instructs CloudFormation to keep the underlying resource when the stack is deleted. The stack itself (the CloudFormation logical resource tracking) is removed from the CloudFormation console, but all physical resources (EC2 instances, RDS databases, S3 buckets, etc.) continue to run and are not affected. The resources become 'orphaned' — running but no longer managed by any CloudFormation stack. This is intentional for protecting stateful resources (databases, S3 buckets with data) from accidental deletion. The deletion is NOT blocked — it proceeds immediately.

stem: A company runs an application that writes to Aurora MySQL. Write latency has been increasing. CloudWatch shows the WriteThroughput metric is consistently at 95% of the maximum. The solutions architect considers adding a read replica to offload writes. Is this a valid solution, and what is the correct approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes — adding a read replica distributes write traffic across multiple nodes

options.label: B
options.text: No — Aurora read replicas cannot accept writes; the correct approach is to enable Aurora Multi-Master (if MySQL version supports it) or shard the.

options.label: C
options.text: No — Aurora read replicas cannot accept writes; to scale writes, scale up the writer instance to a larger instance type or enable Aurora Parallel Query

options.label: D
options.text: Yes — enabling the Aurora writer endpoint load balancing routes writes to the least-loaded replica

explanation: Aurora read replicas ONLY handle read traffic — they cannot accept writes. This is a common misconception. Aurora MySQL Multi-Master was an option for multiple write nodes but is deprecated for new clusters. The architecturally correct answers for write scaling are: (1) Vertical scaling (larger writer instance); (2) Application-level sharding (split the data across multiple Aurora clusters); (3) Rethinking the write pattern (batching, async writes). Aurora Parallel Query improves analytical read query performance, not write throughput. There is no write load balancing across replicas in Aurora MySQL.

stem: A company uses Elastic Beanstalk for a web application. During a deployment, the health check on the ALB fails for the new version. Elastic Beanstalk automatically rolls back. After rollback, the team deploys a fix. This cycle has happened 3 times, causing extended downtime. The team wants to test new versions on a small percentage of traffic before full deployment. Which Elastic Beanstalk feature achieves this?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the traffic splitting deployment policy — send a configurable percentage (e.g., 5%) of traffic

options.label: B
options.text: Use immutable deployments with a longer health check grace period

options.label: C
options.text: Deploy to a separate environment and use weighted Route 53 records for traffic splitting

options.label: D
options.text: Use a Blue/Green deployment by swapping CNAMEs between two Beanstalk environments

explanation: Elastic Beanstalk's traffic splitting deployment is the built-in canary release mechanism: a configurable percentage of requests go to new version instances for an evaluation period; if health metrics pass, the deployment completes; if not, automatic rollback redirects all traffic to the old version. This is faster and more integrated than Blue/Green CNAME swaps. Option A (Blue/Green with CNAME swap) works but switches 100% of traffic atomically, not gradually. Immutable deployment still deploys to all new instances before any rollback. Route 53 weighted records require managing two separate environments.

stem: A company has an application that calls an external payment API. The payment API is occasionally unavailable (1-2% of requests fail with 503). The application currently fails the entire transaction when the API returns 503. The solutions architect needs to implement resilience. Which pattern correctly handles transient API failures?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase the Lambda timeout to 15 minutes to wait for the API to recover

options.label: B
options.text: Cache the API responses in ElastiCache to serve from cache when the API is unavailable

options.label: C
options.text: Implement exponential backoff with jitter in the retry logic; after 3 retries, push the transaction to an SQS queue for later.

options.label: D
options.text: Use SQS FIFO queue for all payment requests to serialize processing and prevent duplicate payments

explanation: Exponential backoff with jitter is the AWS-recommended pattern for handling transient failures: retry after 1s, 2s, 4s (+ random jitter to prevent thundering herd). For requests that still fail after retries, pushing to SQS for later processing provides durability — the transaction is not lost and will be retried when the payment API recovers. Waiting 15 minutes in Lambda is wasteful and exceeds the timeout for synchronous user-facing transactions. FIFO serialization prevents duplicate processing but doesn't handle the 503 transient failure. Caching payment API responses is inappropriate — payment APIs are stateful/transactional and cannot be served from cache.

stem: A company runs a stateful application on a single EC2 instance. The application stores session state in local memory and writes files to the local EBS volume. If the instance fails, both session state and local files are lost. The team wants HA with RPO near zero. Which architectural change achieves this with the LEAST code modification?

options.label: A
options.text: Run the instance in an Auto Scaling group with min=1 max=1; configure user data to restore from the latest EBS snapshot on launch

options.label: B
options.text: Enable EC2 instance recovery in CloudWatch — it recovers the instance on the same host with the same EBS volume

options.label: C
options.text: Use AWS Backup to back up the EBS volume every 5 minutes and restore on failure

options.label: D
options.text: Externalize session state to ElastiCache Redis and move file storage to EFS; then run the application in an ASG with multiple instances

explanation: Externalizing state is the correct HA pattern but the question asks for LEAST code modification. However, option D (EC2 recovery) only recovers from hardware failures (not AZ failures) on the same hardware — this doesn't achieve near-zero RPO if the storage fails. Option C is the architecturally correct answer even though it requires code changes for session externalization and file path changes. The question's correct answer is C — the RPO near zero requires no local state (EFS is multi-AZ shared storage, ElastiCache Redis Multi-AZ keeps session data). All other options have significant RPO or RTO gaps.

stem: A company deploys a new microservice using AWS Lambda. The Lambda function makes synchronous calls to another Lambda function (Function B). During peak load, Function B hits concurrency limits and returns throttling errors to Function A. Function A fails the request. How should the solutions architect redesign this to prevent cascading failures?

options.label: A
options.text: Configure Function A's retry logic to retry Function B calls up to 10 times

options.label: B
options.text: Decouple Function A from Function B using SQS — Function A enqueues requests.

options.label: C
options.text: Use Lambda Provisioned Concurrency on Function B to guarantee available capacity

options.label: D
options.text: Increase Function B's reserved concurrency to unlimited

explanation: Synchronous Lambda-to-Lambda calls create tight coupling and propagate failures (a throttled Function B fails Function A and ultimately the end user). The correct pattern is asynchronous decoupling via SQS: Function A enqueues the work and returns immediately (202 Accepted with a request ID); Function B processes from the queue at its own pace. This prevents cascading failures — queue depth builds up but no requests are lost. Provisioned Concurrency helps with cold starts but doesn't solve the concurrency limit problem (a fixed number of pre-initialized instances). Retries worsen thundering herd when Function B is throttled. Unlimited concurrency could overwhelm downstream dependencies.

stem: A company runs 5 microservices behind a single Application Load Balancer using path-based routing (/service1, /service2, etc.). One service (/payment) has critical availability requirements (99.99%). The other services have 99.9% requirements. Using a single ALB, what is the resilience risk and the recommended mitigation?

options.label: A
options.text: No risk — ALBs are fully managed with 99.99% availability; no mitigation needed

options.label: B
options.text: A single ALB is a correlated failure point — if the ALB fails.

options.label: C
options.text: Use an NLB instead of ALB for the payment service to achieve higher availability

options.label: D
options.text: Enable ALB cross-zone load balancing to prevent AZ-level failures

explanation: While AWS ALBs are highly available, a single ALB is a shared infrastructure component — any configuration errors, deployments, or rare AWS-side issues affect all services. For a service with 99.99% availability requirements, a dedicated ALB provides independent failure isolation: the payment service ALB can have its own security policies, WAF rules, and is not affected by changes to other services' ALB configurations. Cross-zone load balancing prevents AZ imbalance but doesn't isolate failure domains. NLBs have higher throughput but are not inherently more available than ALBs for this purpose.

stem: A company uses AWS Step Functions for a complex workflow. One step calls an external API that takes 10-90 minutes to complete. The Step Functions state polls the external API every minute using a Wait state and a Choice state. This polling costs $0.025 per 1,000 state transitions. With 10,000 workflows per month each polling ~50 times, costs are significant. What architectural pattern eliminates the polling cost?

options.label: A
options.text: Use Step Functions Callback pattern with a taskToken — pause the workflow until the external API calls

options.label: B
options.text: Switch to Lambda for the polling logic to reduce Step Functions state transition costs

options.label: C
options.text: Use EventBridge Scheduler to trigger the Step Functions workflow check every 5 minutes

options.label: D
options.text: Increase the Wait state duration to 5 minutes to reduce polling frequency

explanation: The Step Functions Callback (Wait for Task Token) pattern: the workflow sends a request to the external API including a unique taskToken; the workflow PAUSES (no state transitions while waiting — zero polling cost); when the external API completes, it calls SendTaskSuccess/SendTaskFailure with the taskToken, resuming the workflow. This eliminates all intermediate state transitions. Increasing wait duration reduces but doesn't eliminate polling cost. Lambda polling still costs money for the polling compute. EventBridge Scheduler-triggered checks still incur state transitions for each check.

stem: A company uses Amazon RDS MySQL with Multi-AZ. They want to implement Blue/Green deployment for a schema migration that adds a new column to a high-traffic table. The migration must support rollback within 1 minute if issues arise post-migration. Which RDS feature supports this?

options.label: A
options.text: Use RDS Blue/Green Deployments — create a staging environment (Green) from the production (Blue) database, apply the schema change to Green,

options.label: B
options.text: Create an RDS read replica, apply the schema change, then promote it to primary

options.label: C
options.text: Use AWS DMS to migrate data to a new schema, then perform a CNAME swap

options.label: D
options.text: Take an RDS snapshot, apply the migration to production, restore from snapshot if rollback is needed

explanation: RDS Blue/Green Deployments create a synchronized staging environment (Green) that replicates from the production (Blue). Schema changes are applied to Green; after verification, a switchover (typically <1 minute) redirects traffic to Green. If issues arise, switching back to Blue (the original production) is a <1-minute operation. The Blue environment is maintained post-switchover for rollback. Read replica promotion requires asynchronous replication and doesn't support fast rollback. Snapshot restore takes 20-60 minutes — not <1 minute. DMS introduces complex migration pipelines.

stem: A company deploys Lambda functions using infrastructure as code. They need to be able to roll back a Lambda deployment to a specific previous version in under 30 seconds. The deployment pipeline uses CodeDeploy with Lambda. Which CodeDeploy deployment configuration achieves the fastest potential rollback?

options.label: A
options.text: A custom CodeDeploy configuration with pre/post traffic hooks for health validation

options.label: B
options.text: CodeDeployDefault.LambdaAllAtOnce — deploy to all traffic immediately

options.label: C
options.text: CodeDeployDefault.LambdaCanary10Percent5Minutes — shift 10% traffic, wait 5 minutes, then 100%

options.label: D
options.text: CodeDeployDefault.LambdaLinear10PercentEvery1Minute — gradually shift traffic over 10 minutes

explanation: The question asks about ROLLBACK speed, not deployment speed. All CodeDeploy configurations support automatic rollback (CloudWatch alarm or health check failure) that redirects the Lambda alias back to the previous version — this takes under 30 seconds regardless of deployment config. However, a custom configuration with pre-traffic hooks allows validation BEFORE any production traffic hits the new version, catching issues earlier. The key insight: CodeDeploy rollback for Lambda (alias pointer update) is near-instant regardless of configuration. The real differentiator for rollback protection is the monitoring alarm configuration and hook-based validation.

stem: A company runs a multi-region active-active application. Region A and Region B both process user requests. User data is stored in DynamoDB Global Tables. A network partition event causes Region A and Region B to both process the same user's update independently, resulting in a conflict. How does DynamoDB Global Tables resolve this conflict by default?

options.label: A
options.text: DynamoDB returns an error and requires the application to resolve the conflict manually

options.label: B
options.text: DynamoDB merges conflicting writes by combining all updated attributes into a single item

options.label: C
options.text: DynamoDB uses Last Writer Wins (LWW) based on the item's timestamp attribute — the write with the most recent timestamp is preserved in all replicas

options.label: D
options.text: DynamoDB Global Tables uses Last Writer Wins (LWW) based on the system clock when the write was received, not an item attribute

explanation: DynamoDB Global Tables uses Last Writer Wins (LWW) conflict resolution based on the internal DynamoDB system timestamp (not an application-controlled timestamp attribute). When conflicting writes occur, the write with the highest system-assigned timestamp wins and propagates to all replicas. This is deterministic but can result in data loss if two writes occur very close together. Applications that need custom conflict resolution must implement their own logic (e.g., using DynamoDB Streams to detect and reconcile conflicts). DynamoDB does not merge attributes or require manual resolution.

stem: A company uses CloudFormation StackSets to deploy security baseline resources across 50 member accounts. A new policy requires all accounts to have a specific Config rule. During StackSet deployment, 3 accounts fail with 'FAILED' status while 47 succeed. The administrator needs to understand the impact. Which StackSet behavior and configuration controls failure tolerance?

options.label: A
options.text: StackSet failure tolerance is controlled by MaxConcurrentCount/MaxConcurrentPercentage and FailureToleranceCount/FailureTolerancePercentage — with default settings, failures stop further

options.label: B
options.text: StackSets always deploy all accounts in parallel and failures in some accounts don't affect others

options.label: C
options.text: Failed accounts are automatically retried 3 times before marking as failed

options.label: D
options.text: StackSets roll back all 50 accounts if any account fails

explanation: CloudFormation StackSets deployment behavior: FailureToleranceCount/Percentage determines how many account failures are acceptable before the StackSet operation stops. With default tolerance=0, even 1 failure stops further deployment (remaining queued accounts don't deploy) but already-deployed accounts remain deployed (no rollback). The 47 successful accounts keep their stack instances. The 3 failed accounts have failed stack instances that can be retried. MaxConcurrentCount/Percentage controls how many accounts are processed in parallel. This is important for large-scale deployments — understanding that StackSets don't roll back successful deployments on failure.

stem: A company uses SQS for order processing. The consumer is a fleet of EC2 instances that scale based on queue depth. During a deployment, the new consumer version has a bug and all messages are being processed incorrectly (but not failing — the consumer reports success). The original messages are deleted from SQS. After 10 minutes, the team discovers the bug. How can the company recover the lost messages?

options.label: A
options.text: Restore the SQS queue from the last backup

options.label: B
options.text: Enable SQS FIFO with content-based deduplication to recover the original messages

options.label: C
options.text: Use SQS dead letter queue replay to recover the messages

options.label: D
options.text: SQS messages that have been acknowledged (deleted) cannot be recovered

explanation: SQS messages that are explicitly deleted by the consumer are permanently removed — there is no SQS backup or recovery mechanism for acknowledged messages. This is a fundamental SQS design principle: once deleted, messages are gone. Recovery requires reprocessing from the upstream source (e.g., re-reading from S3, re-sending from the originating application, replaying from Kinesis if the data was also streamed there). This highlights the importance of idempotent processing and maintaining source records. DLQ replay only works for messages that are in the DLQ — messages successfully (incorrectly) processed are gone.

stem: A company has an RDS Aurora PostgreSQL cluster. A long-running analytics query from the application team is locking tables and causing 30-second timeouts for OLTP transactions. Both OLTP and analytics use the same cluster endpoint. What is the correct architectural separation?

options.label: A
options.text: Route OLTP traffic to the writer endpoint and create a dedicated Aurora read replica for analytics.

options.label: B
options.text: Use Aurora Parallel Query to accelerate analytics and reduce lock duration

options.label: C
options.text: Increase the RDS query timeout to allow analytics queries to complete

options.label: D
options.text: Route OLTP traffic to the writer endpoint and analytics queries to the reader endpoint (one of the read replicas)

explanation: The reader endpoint round-robins across all available replicas — if OLTP reads also use the reader endpoint, analytics queries on a shared replica still cause contention for OLTP read traffic. The correct isolation: a dedicated read replica for analytics only (not included in the reader endpoint DNS pool), while OLTP reads use the writer/reader endpoint. This provides complete resource isolation. Aurora Parallel Query improves full-table-scan performance but doesn't eliminate locking contention. Option B is partially correct but shares the reader endpoint — if analytics saturates one replica, OLTP reads on the same replica degrade.

stem: A company runs a critical application with an RTO of 15 minutes and RPO of 5 minutes. The application is in us-east-1 with an RDS Aurora cluster and EC2 instances. Currently, there is no DR configuration. A disaster occurs and us-east-1 is completely unavailable. With the current setup, what is the actual RPO?

options.label: A
options.text: 5 minutes — RDS automated backups run every 5 minutes

options.label: B
options.text: 0 minutes — Aurora stores data in S3 which is region-wide, not AZ-specific

options.label: C
options.text: Up to 24 hours — Aurora PITR data is region-local; without a cross-region replica, the RPO equals all data since the last manual cross-region snapshot

options.label: D
options.text: 1 hour — RDS transaction logs are synced to S3 every hour

explanation: This is a critical AWS architecture trap: Aurora's automated backups and PITR data are stored in S3 within the same region (us-east-1). If the entire region is unavailable, BOTH the database AND its PITR backup data are inaccessible. The actual RPO is the age of the last backup that was cross-region replicated (none, in this scenario). Aurora's data is stored across multiple AZs in S3, but this is within the same region — a full region failure makes it all inaccessible. The correct DR setup for 5-minute RPO would require Aurora Global Database or cross-region automated backup copy.

stem: A company uses AWS Backup for centralized backup management. They configure a backup plan with daily backups (retention 30 days) and weekly backups (retention 1 year) for an RDS Aurora cluster. A compliance audit requires that backups cannot be deleted before the retention period expires, even by administrators. Which AWS Backup feature prevents early backup deletion?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable MFA delete on AWS Backup recovery points

options.label: B
options.text: Attach an SCP that denies backup:DeleteBackupVault to all principals

options.label: C
options.text: Enable versioning on the S3 bucket where AWS Backup stores backups

options.label: D
options.text: Configure AWS Backup Vault Lock in Compliance mode — once locked.

explanation: AWS Backup Vault Lock in Compliance mode is the most powerful protection: once a vault is locked in Compliance mode (after a 72-hour grace period), NO user — including the root account, AWS Support, or any IAM principal — can delete backups before the minimum retention period. The lock cannot be removed. This provides immutable backups for compliance (WORM — Write Once Read Many). S3 versioning is not applicable (Backup uses its own storage). SCPs can be bypassed by the management account. MFA delete exists for S3, not AWS Backup recovery points.

stem: A company uses Amazon ECS with the EC2 launch type. Their Auto Scaling group scales based on EC2 CPU utilization. During scale-in, ECS tasks on terminating instances are abruptly stopped mid-execution, causing data corruption. What ECS feature prevents tasks from being forcefully stopped during scale-in?

options.label: A
options.text: Enable ECS Service Auto Scaling to scale ECS tasks independently of EC2 instances

options.label: B
options.text: Use ECS capacity providers with managed scaling to handle instance termination gracefully

options.label: C
options.text: Configure EC2 Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks to allow ECS to drain tasks from instances before termination

options.label: D
options.text: Enable ECS task protection on long-running tasks using the task protection API

explanation: EC2 Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks pause the termination process at a specific point (e.g., EC2_INSTANCE_TERMINATING) and allow ECS to complete task draining. The hook sends an event to EventBridge/SNS; a Lambda function calls ECS UpdateContainerInstancesState to DRAINING, waits until all tasks are stopped (or their own graceful shutdown completes), then calls CompleteLifecycleAction to allow the termination to proceed. ECS Service Auto Scaling manages task count, not instance termination. Managed scaling via capacity providers helps but the question specifically asks about preventing forceful stopping. Task protection (option D) prevents individual tasks from being stopped by ECS scheduler updates, not EC2 termination.
